C_consistent.finding_title: Synthetic data provenance inconsistency between Phase 2 and Phase 6
C_consistent.requirement_a: Phase 2: 'Real sensor logs (AUVLAB + MOD sea-trial tapes) plus synthetic augmentation for rare events'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 6/Scenario: 'synthetic data provenance chain cannot be verified' triggers pipeline rejection with status synthetic-data-provenance-unverified
C_consistent.conflict_description: Phase 2 assumes synthetic data can be generated and integrated without strict provenance tracking. Yet Phase 6 scenarios explicitly require full lineage verification. This creates a logical gap: Phase 2 generates synthetic data, Phase 6 validates it was generated with provenance. No explicit Phase 2 mandate for 'Sovereign synthetic-data provenance policy' until Section 10.
C_consistent.reasoning: The input source (Section 10) mentions 'Sovereign synthetic-data provenance policy' as a non-AI enabler, suggesting post-hoc adoption. Phase 2 feature does not explicitly state provenance capture during generation. DeFOSPAM identifies this as scenario exception but does not flag it as a Phase 2 requirement gap.
C_consistent.recommendation: Elevate 'Sovereign synthetic-data provenance policy' to a Phase 2 mandatory acceptance criterion; add lineage tracking to StyleGAN/diffusion generation steps; define what constitutes an 'uncertified model snapshot'.

C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 4 / Section 10 & 7.4: 'Human-on-the-loop handovers' and 'Human-on-the-loop operating concept with defined escalation'
C_consistent.conflict_description: Phase 4 mandates both autonomous collision-avoidance AND human-on-the-loop handovers. These are mutually exclusive modes: either the system autonomously avoids collision (human not in loop) or it detects limits and hands over to human. The requirement does not specify when each mode applies or how they coexist.
C_consistent.reasoning: Scenario describes 'human-on-the-loop handover triggered during degraded-sensor mode' — implying autonomous mode requires full sensor suite. But acceptance criterion and Section 10 list 'human-on-the-loop operating concept with defined escalation' as standalone requirement, not conditional on sensor degradation. No explicit ODD boundary between autonomous and human-on-loop operation.
C_consistent.recommendation: Define explicit operating modes: (a) Autonomous mode — conditions (sensor health, ODD bounds); (b) Handover mode — triggers and thresholds; (c) Fallback mode — if handover fails. Map each mode to Phase 4 test scenarios. Add acceptance criterion for mode-transition correctness.

C_consistent.finding_title: Coverage metric ambiguity: '≥95% ODD coverage' vs Phase 2 'rare events'
C_consistent.requirement_a: Section 6 AC: 'Coverage of declared ODD states ≥ 95% (physics-accurate sim + synthetic data)'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 2: 'synthetic augmentation for rare events' (implicitly <5% frequency)
C_consistent.conflict_description: Acceptance criterion targets ≥95% ODD coverage using both real and synthetic data. Yet Phase 2 explicitly scopes synthetic data to 'rare events' (low-frequency). Rare events are by definition outliers. If rare events = <5%, then synthetic data addresses ≤5% of ODD space, leaving 95% to real data. But real AUVLAB corpus alone cannot guarantee 95% coverage.
C_consistent.reasoning: Coverage metric does not specify what baseline (real-only? sim-only? combined?) achieves 95%. If synthetic addresses rare events and real data is limited, the combined coverage may fall short. Scenario: model trained on AUVLAB + synthetic fog/night — does that prove ≥95% ODD coverage, or only ≥95% of declared ODD + synthetic augmentation?
C_consistent.recommendation: Clarify whether ≥95% coverage is measured against (a) declared ODD only, (b) declared ODD + rare-event variants, or (c) the union of all environment variables. Define a quantitative mapping between 'rare events' and % ODD space.

C_consistent.finding_title: Terminology conflict: 'evaluation' vs 'validation' vs 'qualification' across phases
C_consistent.requirement_a: Phase 3: 'Model-layer evaluation'; Phase 5: 'Real-world validation'; Phase 7: 'continuous re-qualification'
C_consistent.requirement_b: PSRQ defined as 'formal evaluation campaign'; Section 7: 'evaluation campaign a USV must pass'
C_consistent.conflict_description: Terms are used interchangeably without IEEE/DEFSTAN definitions. Phase 3 = 'evaluation' (lab), Phase 5 = 'validation' (real-world), Phase 7 = 'qualification' (repeated). PSRQ is called both 'evaluation campaign' and 'qualification'. No explicit standard invoked (JSP 520, DEFSTAN 00-056, RA 1600 mentioned but not cited per phase).
C_consistent.reasoning: IEEE 1012 / DEFSTAN 00-056 distinguish verification (built right) vs validation (right thing). Phase 3 is verification, Phase 5 is validation, Phase 7 is re-qualification. But glossary defines PSRQ as 'formal evaluation campaign' without distinguishing these. Safety case (Section 6) requires AMLAS evidence tied to specific lifecycle activities — terminology mismatch may break evidence mapping.
C_consistent.recommendation: Adopt explicit standards: Phase 3 = Verification (V&V standard), Phase 5 = Validation (per DEFSTAN 00-056 or RA 1600), Phase 7 = Re-qualification. Map each phase name to a specific AMLAS activity and defence standard. Update glossary.

C_consistent.finding_title: Logical conflict: 'Closed-loop simulation replaces most physical trials' vs 'anchor synthetic evidence'
C_consistent.requirement_a: Section 8 / AI benefits: 'Closed-loop simulation replaces most physical trials'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 5: 'Targeted physical trials to anchor the synthetic evidence — weather, comms and UK-specific littoral traffic'
C_consistent.conflict_description: Section 8 claims simulation replaces 'most' physical trials, implying minimal real-world testing. Yet Phase 5 explicitly requires 'targeted physical trials to anchor synthetic evidence.' These are contradictory: either simulation enables skipping most trials, or sim-to-real gap mandates substantive trial campaigns.
C_consistent.reasoning: The 10× scenario productivity claim (Section 8) and 'closed-loop simulation replaces most physical trials' are aspirational benefits tied to synthetic data. But Phase 5 scenario 'Real-world validation mismatch: model degrades to 71% mAP on unseen sea-trial data' shows sim-to-real gap is real and requires 'targeted' real-world trials. 'Most' ≠ 'all'; the phrase is ambiguous.
C_consistent.recommendation: Quantify 'most': e.g., 'simulation-based testing can replace 70% of baseline manual trial campaigns, with 30% reserved for coastal-weather and adversarial-scenario anchoring.' Update Phase 5 acceptance criteria to specify minimum sea-trial scale (e.g., ≥3 weather windows, ≥50 encounter scenarios, ≥2 geographic regions).

C_consistent.finding_title: Temporal conflict: 'Drift monitors live on ≥3 environment variables post-deployment' without Phase 7 baseline definition
C_consistent.requirement_a: Section 6 AC: 'Drift monitors live on ≥3 environment variables post-deployment'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 7 scenarios: 'drift monitor on sea-state variable triggers re-qualification alert' and 'visibility mean drifts from 300 m to 310 m at 95% CI'
C_consistent.conflict_description: AC specifies drift monitors on ≥3 variables but does not define (a) baseline distribution, (b) drift thresholds, (c) re-qualification trigger semantics, or (d) continuous vs episodic monitoring. Phase 7 scenarios show specific KS test (P<0.05) and CI-based thresholds, but these are not reconciled with AC or mapped to safety-case evidence levels.
C_consistent.reasoning: Scenario shows 'visibility mean drifts from 300 m to 310 m at 95% CI boundary' → SLO-yellow alert. But AC does not define SLO thresholds. Is ±10% acceptable? Who sets it? Does re-qualification depend on magnitude or duration of drift? Phase 7 feature mentions 'Evidently AI, Alibi Detect, Arize' but each tool uses different drift metrics (KS, Kolmogorov-Smirnov, MMD, Jensen-Shannon). AC must specify which metric(s) are authoritative.
C_consistent.recommendation: Phase 1 must decompose 'drift monitors' into (a) measurable environment variables with baseline distributions, (b) drift metrics (specify: KS test, MMD, other), (c) thresholds per variable (±10%? σ-based?), (d) escalation rules (yellow → red → halt). Add Phase 7 AC: 'Drift detection SLO ≥95% sensitivity at specified threshold, ≤5% false-positive rate.' Map to AMLAS Activity 6 (in-service monitoring).

C_consistent.finding_title: Cross-phase scope conflict: System-layer (Phase 4) vs Real-world (Phase 5) — what is tested where?
C_consistent.requirement_a: Phase 4 (System-layer): 'HIL and digital-twin scenarios — closed-loop collision-avoidance, human-on-the-loop handovers, degraded-sensor modes'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 5 (Real-world): 'Targeted physical trials to anchor the synthetic evidence — weather, comms and UK-specific littoral traffic'
C_consistent.conflict_description: Phase 4 tests collision-avoidance and handovers in HIL/digital-twin (simulated). Phase 5 tests same systems in real-world. Scope boundary is undefined: does Phase 4 handover failure halt Phase 5, or can Phase 5 proceed with caveats? Does Phase 5 re-test all Phase 4 scenarios or only select 'targeted trials'?
C_consistent.reasoning: Scenario 'Real-world validation mismatch: model degrades to 71% mAP on unseen sea-trial data' shows Phase 4 qualification is not sufficient. But acceptance criteria do not mandate Phase 5 to re-qualify Phase 4 findings. If Phase 4 shows 92% mAP (well-calibrated) and Phase 5 shows 71% mAP, what happens to the safety case? Does Phase 4 evidence become invalidated?
C_consistent.recommendation: Define Phase 4 vs Phase 5 scope explicitly: (a) Phase 4 = end-to-end functionality in controlled sim; (b) Phase 5 = subset of Phase 4 scenarios in realistic weather/traffic; (c) Phase 5 must achieve ≥80% of Phase 4 accuracy or trigger design re-work. Add acceptance criterion: 'Phase 5 shall re-qualify ≥3 Phase 4 edge cases (degraded-sensor, high-traffic, extreme weather) with sim-to-real delta <20% mAP loss.'

C_consistent.finding_title: Adversarial robustness scope inconsistency between Phase 3 and tooling list
C_consistent.requirement_a: Phase 3 / AC: 'Adversarial robustness bounds filed for every class'; Feature: 'IBM ART, Foolbox and CleverHans'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Section 9 tools: 'Garak / PyRIT for red-teaming'; Section 3 scenario: Garak identifies jailbreak in 'LLM subsystem for multi-vessel encounter reasoning'
C_consistent.conflict_description: Phase 3 adversarial robustness feature targets 'object class' robustness (e.g., ship detection, small-boat). But tooling (Garak, PyRIT) and scenarios address adversarial prompts and LLM jailbreaks — a different attack surface. AC does not clarify whether adversarial robustness includes LLM/NLP security or only perception-stack robustness (CNN/detector).
C_consistent.reasoning: If collision-avoidance logic includes an LLM reasoning component (per scenario), robustness bounds must include prompt-injection risks. IBM ART and Foolbox are designed for image classifiers; Garak is for LLMs. The AC 'bounds filed for every class' does not distinguish object classes (radar/visual objects) from LLM input classes (multi-vessel reasoning, beacon interpretation).
C_consistent.recommendation: Split adversarial robustness into two phases: (1) Phase 3 perception layer: IBM ART/Foolbox for sensor-domain adversarial robustness (image patches, sensor spoofing); (2) Phase 3 decision layer: Garak/PyRIT for NLP/reasoning-domain robustness (prompt injection, beacon-text spoofing). Define 'every class' to include both modalities. Add AC: 'LLM-based decision components tested against ≥10 adversarial prompt templates per encounter scenario.'

C_consistent.finding_title: Standards alignment gap: JSP 520 vs DEFSTAN 00-056 vs RA 1600 — no primary standard cited
C_consistent.requirement_a: Section 10: 'Alignment with JSP 520, JSP 936, DEFSTAN 00-056, RA 1600 and the UK Defence AI Playbook'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Acceptance criteria and features reference AMLAS, GSN, Article 36 but do not cite specific defence standards
C_consistent.conflict_description: Section 10 lists four UK defence standards but does not establish a precedence hierarchy or mapping to PSRQ phases. JSP 520 (defence equipment), JSP 936 (project management), DEFSTAN 00-056 (safety), RA 1600 (robustness/assurance) — each covers different domains. No feature or AC explicitly invokes one standard as primary or resolves conflicts if they arise.
C_consistent.reasoning: If DEFSTAN 00-056 and RA 1600 both define 'qualification,' which applies? If JSP 520 and AMLAS define data governance differently, which takes precedence? SACE is mentioned in glossary as 'methodology for building safety assurance cases' but is not mapped to any defence standard. DeFOSPAM findings do not flag this.
C_consistent.recommendation: Create a standards matrix: Phase × Standard → Applicable sections. For example: (Phase 5, DEFSTAN 00-056) = 'Safety management for operational trials'; (Phase 6, RA 1600) = 'Assurance and risk'. Designate one standard (likely DEFSTAN 00-056 or RA 1600) as primary for safety evidence. Resolve conflicts explicitly.

C_consistent.finding_title: Characteristic conflict: 'rare events' provenance described as generation problem, not labelling problem
C_consistent.requirement_a: Phase 2 / Scenario: 'synthetic data provenance chain cannot be verified' leads to rejection when 'offline generation omits lineage'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 2 / Real-world scenario: 'AUVLAB corpus ingested, metadata extracted and classified'; diffusion inpainting scenario: 'the generated sector is flagged as synthetic and excluded from ground-truth labelling'
C_consistent.conflict_description: Provenance problem is framed as lineage tracking during generation (StyleGAN, diffusion) but not during curation. Real AUVLAB data ingestion extracts 'linked provenance and sensor calibration records' — implying provenance is a metadata+labelling concern. Yet synthetic data provenance exception focuses on generation model snapshot certification.
C_consistent.reasoning: Diffusion inpainting scenario shows inpainted radar sectors must be 'flagged as synthetic and excluded from ground-truth labelling.' This is a labelling/curation step, not a generation-time provenance issue. But the exception in the previous scenario rejects synthetic data when 'offline generation omits lineage' — suggesting generation-time provenance is the concern. These are orthogonal issues: (a) generation model certification (is StyleGAN trustworthy?), (b) instance-level flagging (is this pixel synthetic?).
C_consistent.recommendation: Separate concerns: (1) Generation Provenance (Phase 2a) — certify model snapshots via MLOps pipeline; (2) Instance Labelling (Phase 2b) — tag synthetic pixels at curation time; (3) Evidence Chain (Phase 6) — link synthetic instances to generation model and confidence bounds. Update Phase 2 acceptance criteria to cover both.

C_consistent.finding_title: Logical conflict: 'human-on-the-loop operating concept with defined escalation' vs automated re-qualification
C_consistent.requirement_a: Section 10: 'Human-on-the-loop operating concept with defined escalation'
C_consistent.requirement_b: Phase 7 / Section 8: 'In-service drift monitoring replaces periodic re-qualification'; Feature: 'trigger re-qualification and model updates automatically post-deployment'
C_consistent.conflict_description: Human-on-the-loop requires human escalation and approval. Yet Phase 7 mandates 'automatic' re-qualification and 'automatic' model updates on drift detection. These are mutually exclusive: either a human approves re-qualification (human-on-the-loop) or the system automatically re-qualifies (fully autonomous).
C_consistent.reasoning: Phase 7 scenario: 'Negative: human operator ignores drift alert, continues operation beyond SLO' → Article 36 exception raised. This implies human *can* override automatic escalation, but only if they acknowledge the risk. The feature description says 'automatically post-deployment' but scenarios show human approval gates. No explicit rule for when automation vs human approval applies.
C_consistent.recommendation: Define escalation levels: (1) SLO-yellow drift → automatic logging, no action; (2) SLO-red drift → automatic alert + human acknowledgement required within 2 hours; (3) Critical drift (e.g., new sensor malfunction) → automatic system halt pending re-qualification. Update Section 8 to clarify 'automatic' means 'automatically detected' not 'automatically remediated without human approval.' Add human-on-the-loop SLA to Phase 7 AC.

R.finding_title: Phase 7 decomposition incomplete: drift detection, alert escalation, and re-qualification triggering must separate into sub-features
R.reasoning: REQ-013 (Live drift detection) aggregates three distinct operational concerns: (1) drift metric computation on 3 environment variables (Beaufort scale, visibility, sea clutter), (2) multi-level alert escalation (local threshold breach → Dstl team notification → auto-suppress if unresolved within 12 hours), (3) downstream re-qualification triggering in MLOps pipeline. DeFOSPAM Phase 1 identified decomposition needed. These are separate contracts with different SLOs and governance approvals. Grouping them masks risk: e.g., alert escalation may slip independently of drift sensing.
R.recommendation: Decompose REQ-013 into: REQ-013a (Drift metric computation), REQ-013b (Alert escalation and suppression SOP), REQ-013c (Re-qualification triggering and evidence auto-sync). Assign to v1.0 (drift sensing only, manual re-qualification), v1.1 (auto-escalation, auto-suppress), and v1.2 (full re-qualification pipeline). Clarify Dstl approval authority for each sub-feature. Update AMLAS Phase 7 decomposition in safety case.

R.finding_title: No feature addresses 'Data Lifecycle & Compliance' (data retention, destruction, re-use policy for synthetic data) — critical gap for regulatory audit
R.reasoning: DeFOSPAM Phase 3 (Milarna) identified missing non-functional requirement: 'Data lifecycle and evidence retention — no archival, destruction, or re-use policy for classified synthetic data.' No feature in the 16 explicitly addresses: (a) retention period for classified synthetic training/test data (how long kept on MOD DAIS/Crown Hosting?), (b) destruction SOP when model is retired, (c) re-use permissions across multiple USV programmes (can Programme A synthetic data be transferred to Programme B?), (d) DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) attestation for synthetic datasets. This is a non-negotiable regulatory requirement for UK Defence. Absence will trigger Dstl compliance finding at Phase 6.
R.recommendation: Create new feature REQ-017 (Data Lifecycle & Compliance Management): 'The system shall define and enforce data retention, destruction, and re-use policies for classified synthetic training/test data and evidence artifacts. Policies shall include: (a) Retention: classified synthetic data retained ≤5 years per MOD DAIS/Crown Hosting policy, with extension authority (Dstl Security Officer). Evidence store retained indefinitely for audit/appeal. (b) Destruction: certified deletion with audit trail when programme concludes or model superseded; SOP defined in AMLAS Phase 2 and approved by Dstl Security Officer. (c) Re-use: synthetic data generated with classified input cannot be transferred across programmes without explicit Dstl DAIS governance approval. (d) DPIA: mandatory attestation that synthetic-data provenance and retention policies satisfy GDPR/UK-DPA for any real personal data inadvertently captured in training corpus. (e) Backup/Recovery: evidence store RTO ≤4 hours, RPO ≤1 hour (critical for regulatory audit).' Assign to Safety Engineer + Dstl Security Officer for co-authorship. Target v1.0 (policy definition) + v1.1 (automated enforcement in MLOps store).

R.finding_title: Synthetic data provenance enforcement is missing from v1.0 scope: no hard gate blocks offline generation
R.reasoning: REQ-003 (Synthetic sensor data generation) specifies full provenance preservation and MLflow integration, but DeFOSPAM finding on 'provenance chain unverifiable when offline generation omits lineage' (confidence 10, CRITICAL) indicates the enforcement mechanism is unclear. v1.0 lacks a hard gate in the evidence curation pipeline to reject synthetic batches without MLflow tracking. Without this gate, a team member could offline-generate 5000 images (uncertified model, no lineage) and attempt import, breaking Dstl audit trail post-hoc. This is a qualification blocker if Dstl discovers provenance gaps at Phase 6 sign-off.
R.recommendation: Define a hard validation gate in the evidence curation pipeline (Phase 2/3 boundary) that rejects synthetic batches missing: model ID, version, hyperparameters, seed, generation timestamp, CRC. Integrate this gate into the CI/CD pipeline (REQ-016). Add test case: 'Given uncertified offline synthetic batch, when curation import is attempted, then import rejected with 'synthetic-data-provenance-unverified' error.' Document acceptable provenance standards in AMLAS Activity Evidence Summary template. Assign ownership to MLOps Lead for v1.0 implementation.

R.finding_title: CI/CD pipeline (REQ-016) depends on MLOps lineage (REQ-014) and evidence store (REQ-011) but dependencies not declared in feature specs
R.reasoning: REQ-016 (CI/CD pipeline integration) assumes that REQ-014 (MLOps lineage) and REQ-011 (evidence auto-generation) are operational prerequisites. If REQ-014 lineage tracking is delayed or REQ-011 GSN auto-generation produces unvalidated output, the full CI/CD pipeline (Phase 3 automated evaluation → Phase 4 HIL results → evidence aggregation → GSN generation) breaks. However, the ranking does not declare these dependencies or prioritize delivery sequence. Risk: team begins REQ-016 implementation before REQ-014 and REQ-011 are production-ready, creating integration debt.
R.recommendation: Declare explicit dependency chain: REQ-014 (MLOps lineage) → REQ-011 (evidence aggregation) → REQ-016 (CI/CD pipeline). Sequence implementation in Phase 3: (1) Month 1-2: REQ-014 (MLOps lineage tracking, MLflow integration), (2) Month 2-3: REQ-011 (SACE/GSN auto-generation skeleton), (3) Month 3-4: REQ-016 (full CI/CD pipeline integration). Assign ownership: MLOps Lead (REQ-014), Safety Engineer (REQ-011), DevOps/SRE (REQ-016). Update technical roadmap and AMLAS phase schedule to reflect sequencing.

R.finding_title: Red-teaming methodology (REQ-006) volatile: Garak/PyRIT frameworks rapidly evolving, v1.0 scope at risk
R.reasoning: REQ-006 specifies Garak and PyRIT red-teaming frameworks (late 2024 tools). These are high-velocity projects with monthly API updates and new attack vectors emerging (adversarial methods literature growing). While REQ-005 (adversarial robustness) targets stable metrics (Foolbox/CleverHans), REQ-006 relies on frameworks that may introduce breaking changes during Phase 3 (6-month window). If Garak v0.11 API breaks compatibility mid-Phase 3, team must either backport tests or delay red-teaming. Dstl approvers may also demand newer attack methods if threat landscape shifts (e.g., diffusion-model adversarial perturbations emerge).
R.recommendation: Classify REQ-006 as 'conditional should_have' with strict dependency on framework API stability. Define acceptance criteria: 'Red-teaming methodology locked to Garak v0.10+, PyRIT v0.6+ by Phase 3 kick-off. Any framework updates post-lock require safety engineer re-approval.' Alternatively, defer REQ-006 to v1.1 in-service red-teaming (post-Phase 5, after real-world adversarial observations available). For v1.0, rely on REQ-005 adversarial bounds + simpler scenario-based testing (REQ-009). Assign volatility tracking to MLOps Lead.
R.recommendation_alt: If v1.0 timeline cannot slip: pin framework versions in Docker/containerized CI/CD; establish compatibility test suite for each framework pre-release. Engage Dstl security engineer to define red-teaming SOP (which attacks mandatory vs. optional) early in Phase 3.

R.finding_title: REQ-013 (drift detection) v1.1 target risks delaying continuous re-qualification capability beyond first operational release
R.reasoning: REQ-013 ranked as 'essential' but 'v1.1 target' (in-service monitoring). This creates a gap: v1.0 PSRQ sign-off will not include post-deployment drift monitoring. If the model begins to degrade in the field during Phase 7 (first 6 months at sea), there is no automated alert or re-qualification trigger until v1.1 lands. Manual human monitoring is fallback, but creates operational risk and Dstl liability exposure. UK Defence may refuse to sign off v1.0 without some drift-detection capability (even basic form).
R.recommendation: Reconsider v1.0 scope: either (a) include simplified drift detection in v1.0 (3-variable monitoring on recorded logs, 6-hour batch uploads, human-triggered re-qualification workflow), or (b) explicitly document in AMLAS Safety Case that Phase 7 is 'manual monitoring only' pending v1.1 software update. Coordinate with Dstl PSRQ lead to confirm acceptability of v1.0 without live drift detection. If unacceptable, escalate Phase 3-6 timeline compression or descope lower-priority features (e.g., defer REQ-006 red-teaming to v1.1).

R.finding_title: Explainability (REQ-007) ranked 'conditional' but safety-case narrative may demand it for Dstl approval
R.reasoning: REQ-007 (explainability/interpretability) ranked as 'conditional should_have', implying Dstl can accept a safety case without SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM reports. However, DeFOSPAM Phase 1 and UK Defence AI Playbook guidance suggest explainability is increasingly expected in safety-critical autonomous systems. If Dstl safety engineer encounters unexplained failure modes in Phase 4 HIL results (e.g., 'model missed small vessel in clutter 8 times'), they may demand explainability analysis before Phase 4 gate approval. This could convert REQ-007 from conditional to essential mid-phase.
R.recommendation: Conduct early (Phase 2) scoping conversation with Dstl PSRQ lead: 'Is explainability (SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM) mandatory for AMLAS Phase 4 gate sign-off, or can we substitute with conservative ODD restrictions and manual failure-mode analysis?' Document decision in AMLAS Activity Evidence Summary template. If mandatory, promote REQ-007 to 'essential must_have v1.0'. If optional, maintain 'conditional should_have' but add contingency: if Phase 4 reveals unexplained failures, explainability work triggers immediately.

R.finding_title: Article 36 legal review (REQ-012) underspecified: no SLA for legal turnaround or rejection remediation process
R.reasoning: REQ-012 specifies Article 36 evidence export and Dstl legal office submission but does not define: (a) legal review SLA (e.g., 'UK Defence legal office shall complete review within 10 working days'), (b) rejection criteria (what legal objections are expected?), (c) remediation SOP (if legal rejects, how quickly must evidence be updated?), (d) re-submission cycles. AMLAS Phase 6 schedule assumes legal approval as a gate, but without SLAs, legal review could block v1.0 indefinitely. Phase 5 (targeted physical trials) completion is also gated on Phase 6 legal approval per the requirement, creating circular dependency risk.
R.recommendation: Revise REQ-012 to include: (a) Legal review SLA: Dstl legal office review completed within 15 working days of submission; (b) Rejection process: if legal objects, provision for up to 2 revision cycles, each with 5-working-day remediation window; (c) Escalation: if legal review unresolved after 2 cycles, escalate to UK Defence General Counsel for expedited review (10-day SLA); (d) Accept/reject decision driver (compliance with JSP 520, Defence AI Playbook, IHL/LOAC). Coordinate SLA with Dstl Programme Manager and legal office pre-Phase 1. Document in AMLAS Phase 6 stage-gate approval criteria.

V.finding_title: Phase 2 representative data claim lacks ODD coverage definition
V.original_requirement: I can ensure Phase 2 datasets are representative and fully traced
V.verifiable_rewrite: By Phase 2 completion, the system shall populate ≥228 out of 240 declared ODD cells (4 key dimensions: sea-state 2–4, visibility 3 bins, vessel-class 2 variants, sensor-mode 2–3 options) with ≥1 real sensor log each, all tagged with ODD metadata and SHA256 hash.
V.acceptance_criterion: At Phase 2 gate, when the ODD coverage audit is run, then ≥228 ODD cells (95.0% of 240) shall each contain ≥1 real sensor log with metadata (timestamps, signal-to-noise ratio, frame rate) and file hash. Fail if <228 cells populated or any log missing SNR or hash. Evidence: MLOps dataset inventory report.
V.reasoning: 'Fully traced' is unbounded—it could mean 'all logs have metadata' or 'all ODD cells covered'. The requirement specifies ≥228/240 cells, which is quantified and verifiable. Without an explicit ODD envelope, the criterion cannot be binary.
V.recommendation: Explicitly define the 4-dimensional ODD envelope (sea-state ∈ {Beaufort 2,3,4}, visibility ∈ {50 m, 200 m, 500 m}, etc.) in Phase 1 requirements. Lock the envelope definition before Phase 2 data ingestion.

V.finding_title: Adversarial robustness bounds reference undefined Navy baseline
V.original_requirement: all bounds meet Navy-defined robustness margins
V.verifiable_rewrite: Adversarial robustness bounds (accuracy degradation under FGSM, PGD, C&W attacks with perturbation budgets 10% and 20%) shall be filed and reviewed against a pre-agreed Navy baseline. For now, bounds shall satisfy: (1) accuracy drop ≤20% under ε=0.1 (10% pixel range), (2) accuracy drop ≤40% under ε=0.2 (20% pixel range). These limits apply to all 5 object classes.
V.acceptance_criterion: When adversarial robustness bounds are computed for each class (vessel, buoy, small-object, small-contact, debris) under 3 attack methods (FGSM, PGD, C&W) and 2 perturbation budgets (ε=0.1, ε=0.2), then (1) each class shall show accuracy drop ≤20% at ε=0.1, (2) each class shall show accuracy drop ≤40% at ε=0.2. Fail if any class exceeds these thresholds for any attack method. Bounds filed in SACE XML before Phase 4 gate.
V.reasoning: 'Navy-defined' is a forward reference to a non-existent specification. The requirement must either cite an existing Navy standard (not found in scope) or establish baseline limits in the SRS itself.
V.recommendation: Create a separate Dstl/Navy robustness specification document (e.g., 'RAN Perception Stack Robustness Baseline v1.0') and reference it by version. Version this document and update the SRS requirement to cite the specific version.

V.finding_title: Article 36 legal review process lacks pass/fail criteria
V.original_requirement: Legal review shall assess: (a) compliance with UK autonomous systems frameworks (JSP 520, Defence AI Playbook), (b) adequacy of human oversight and intervention points, (c) liability and attribution for autonomous decisions. Legal review approval gate must be passed before PSRQ sign-off
V.verifiable_rewrite: Article 36 legal review shall assess three dimensions: (1) Regulatory Compliance: evidence bundle maps to all required JSP 520 + Defence AI Playbook checkpoints (list: model card, data provenance, robustness bounds, human handover points, failure modes). (2) Human Oversight: collision-avoidance stack includes human-in-the-loop override mechanism with response time <2 s; all autonomous decisions logged with operator visibility. (3) Liability: system design documents liability model (who decides if autonomous action fails? is there insurance?). Review outcome: PASS (all 3 dimensions green) or FAIL (≥1 dimension red, RCA required). Gate opens only on PASS.
V.acceptance_criterion: Before PSRQ Phase 6 sign-off, Article 36 legal review shall assess: (1) Regulatory Compliance: inspect SACE XML and model card against JSP 520 v3.0 + Defence AI Playbook v2.0 requirement lists (mapped in a compliance matrix). Pass if ≥95% of checkpoints addressed. (2) Human Oversight: test human override response time on ≥10 scenarios; pass if ≥95% of overrides respond <2 s. Log all autonomous decisions and verify operator visibility (telemetry stream includes decision rationale). (3) Liability: review system architecture document for explicit liability assignment (e.g., 'OnWatch Engineer is liable if override not executed'). Pass if liability model is documented and unambiguous. Final gate: PASS if all 3 dimensions pass; FAIL if any red, requiring RCA + remediation before re-assessment.
V.reasoning: Legal review is mandatory but criteria are vague ('adequacy of human oversight'). Pass/fail must be objective: compliance against a checklist (JSP 520), measurable human response time, and explicit liability assignment in design docs.
V.recommendation: Create a 'Legal Review Assessment Checklist' mapping JSP 520 + Defence AI Playbook requirements to the SRS features. Define PASS as ≥95% checklist items addressed, with cross-references to evidence artifacts (model card, test results, architecture doc).

V.finding_title: Synthetic data realism criterion lacks measurable validation threshold
V.verifiable_rewrite: Synthetic sensor data (radar echoes, camera images, LiDAR pointclouds) shall pass visual QA: human reviewer shall certify that ≥95% of synthetic frames are indistinguishable from real sensor data in ≥3 out of 4 ODD conditions (fog, calm sea, merchant traffic, daylight). Domain-adaptation realism score (LPIPS or FID) shall be ≤0.15 vs. real reference set.
V.acceptance_criterion: For each batch of 1000 synthetic images generated, when a qualified human reviewer (maritime sensor SME, blinded to synthetic vs. real labels) scores 100 randomly sampled frames, then ≥95 frames shall be rated 'physics-plausible' on a 5-point Likert scale (≥4/5). Additionally, FID (Frechet Inception Distance) between synthetic and real distributions shall be ≤0.15. Fail if human rating <95% or FID >0.15.
V.reasoning: 'Physics-accurate' is qualitative. Realism can be measured via human perception studies (Likert scale) and ML-based distribution metrics (FID, LPIPS). Both are binary once thresholds are set.
V.recommendation: Run human evaluation studies on a representative sample of synthetic data early in Phase 3. Use FID as a rapid, automated proxy for realism in CI/CD pipeline.

V.finding_title: Decomposition effort baseline lacks objective reference
V.original_requirement: T&E Engineer effort shall decrease from 10 engineer-days (manual decomposition baseline) to 2–3 engineer-days
V.verifiable_rewrite: For a medium-scale USV perception stack (≥50 top-level requirements), the decomposition tool shall complete structured requirements parsing in ≤24 elapsed hours, with ≤2 engineer-days of human review effort required
V.acceptance_criterion: Given 50+ top-level requirements in SRS format, when the decomposition pipeline executes, then (1) atomic clause extraction completes within 4 hours, (2) traceability matrix generation within 6 hours, (3) human review and audit log annotation completed within 2 calendar days, with ≤3 defects per 50 requirements (95% confidence)
V.reasoning: The baseline '10 engineer-days' is historical and may vary with team skill, requirement clarity, and tools used. Without an objective, repeatable definition of 'engineer-day' (e.g., 8-hour shift, task scope), the criterion is not binary pass/fail. Test: measure actual effort on a canonical 50-requirement dataset.
V.recommendation: Define 'engineer-day' as 1 full-time equivalent working on decomposition with standard tools. Establish a reference SRS with 50+ requirements and baseline effort on 3 trials to set repeatable target.

V.finding_title: Scenario authoring productivity gain lacks repeatable measurement baseline
V.original_requirement: design throughput shall increase from baseline ~1 scenario/engineer-day (manual OpenSCENARIO XML) to ≥10 scenarios/engineer-day
V.verifiable_rewrite: Scenario authoring throughput shall be measured on a canonical test set: 20 scenario templates (vessel encounters, traffic regimes, weather transitions) covering ≥4 ODD cells each. Baseline: manual OpenSCENARIO XML authoring. Target: AI-assisted Scenic DSL + auto-compilation. Throughput = scenarios completed + accepted per engineer-day (8 h, inclusive of review). Target: ≥10 scenarios/engineer-day on the canonical set.
V.acceptance_criterion: In Phase 3, the T&E team shall author 20 reference scenarios on the canonical ODD set using Scenic + templates. Measure: (1) time to author + compile (automated), (2) time for human review + acceptance (manual), (3) total elapsed time, (4) scenarios completed per 8-hour engineer-day. Target: ≥10 scenarios/day on average over 5 authoring sessions. Pass if mean throughput ≥10 scenarios/engineer-day with ≥3 independent trials. Fail if <10 scenarios/engineer-day or >20% variance between trials.
V.reasoning: 'Baseline ~1 scenario/engineer-day' is cited but not rigorously defined. Without a canonical test set and repeatable measurement protocol, the claim is not verifiable. The '10×' improvement is comparative but lacks context (which tool versions? which engineer skill levels?).
V.recommendation: Establish a 'Scenario Authoring Baseline Study' in Phase 2: have team members author 10 scenarios manually on the canonical set, measure time, and record variance. Use this as the repeatable baseline for Phase 3 comparison.

V.finding_title: Decomposition auditability criterion lacks measurable coverage threshold
V.original_requirement: All decomposition decisions shall be auditable (tool rationale logged for each row)
V.verifiable_rewrite: For each atomic clause generated by the decomposition tool, the system shall log and retain a timestamped audit entry including: (1) source requirement ID, (2) clause extraction rule applied, (3) mapping to AMLAS technique, (4) human reviewer approval/rejection with timestamp. Audit completeness ≥99% of rows.
V.acceptance_criterion: Given any decomposition run outputting N clauses, when an auditor samples ≥100 rows uniformly at random from the traceability matrix, then ≥99 rows (99%) shall have a complete, timestamped audit log with rule identifier and reviewer sign-off. Fail if any row lacks timestamp or rule reference.
V.reasoning: 'Auditable' is subjective—does it mean 'can be traced' or 'meets regulatory standard'? A pass/fail criterion requires quantifying coverage and completeness. Inspection of audit logs is the natural verification method.
V.recommendation: Mandate a structured audit schema (JSON with required fields: timestamp, clause_id, rule_id, reviewer, decision). Enforce schema validation at write-time in MLOps database.

V.finding_title: ODD stratification analysis lacks definition of 'rare' cells
V.original_requirement: no class exhibits >10% accuracy drop in rare ODD cells
V.verifiable_rewrite: For each object class (vessel, buoy, small-object, small-contact, debris) and each ODD cell where ≥5 test samples exist, the drop in mAP from the macro-average shall not exceed 10 percentage points. Rare cells (containing 5–20 test samples) are flagged for monitoring but included in the pass/fail criterion.
V.acceptance_criterion: When the Phase 3 model-layer evaluation computes per-class, per-ODD-cell mAP@IoU=0.5, then for any ODD cell containing ≥5 held-out test samples, the accuracy (per-class mAP) shall not drop >10 pp below the macro-average (average across all ODD cells). Report per-cell results stratified by class. Fail if any class in any cell with ≥5 samples drops >10 pp.
V.reasoning: 'Rare' is undefined—does it mean <5 samples? <20? The specification should quantify the threshold for inclusion in the pass/fail criterion vs. 'monitoring-only' cells.
V.recommendation: Define thresholds: (1) 'rare cell' = 5–20 samples, (2) 'insufficient data' = <5 samples (excluded from criterion, included in report as notes), (3) 'nominal cell' = ≥20 samples. Set a 10 pp tolerance band with confidence intervals.

V.finding_title: Red-teaming scenario intensity lacks quantified coverage
V.original_requirement: scenarios that cause accuracy drop >30% shall trigger investigation
V.verifiable_rewrite: Adversarial scenarios (sensor spoofing, denial-of-service, occlusion, object morphing) that cause model accuracy to drop >30 pp below baseline clean accuracy shall be logged in the adversarial scenario registry. For each such scenario, the root cause shall be analyzed: (1) was the ODD boundary exceeded? (2) is the failure mode known and acceptable? (3) does algorithm hardening or ODD restriction mitigate it?
V.acceptance_criterion: Given ≥20 unique adversarial scenarios tested in Phase 3 (spoofing, jamming, occlusion, morphing), when the system logs accuracy degradation for each scenario, then any scenario causing >30 pp accuracy drop shall trigger a formal root-cause investigation. Investigation outcome (decision to harden, restrict ODD, or accept) shall be documented in the scenario registry within 3 working days. Fail if investigation missing or unresolved after 5 working days.
V.reasoning: 'Stress-test' and 'trigger investigation' are action-dependent, not binary. The criterion must quantify (1) number of scenarios, (2) what counts as a 'hit' (>30% drop), (3) investigation process and timeline.
V.recommendation: Establish a formal red-teaming process: scenario submission → automated eval → hit detection → root cause analysis → decision → documentation. Assign SLA (3 working days for analysis, 5 for decision).

V.finding_title: Explainability audit trail completeness lacks failure-mode threshold
V.original_requirement: Any unexplained failure modes (>5% of failure cases) shall trigger model re-training or ODD restriction
V.verifiable_rewrite: Explainability audit trail shall be generated for ≥95% of all test samples (real and synthetic). A failure case is 'explained' if human safety engineer confirms that SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM features align with expected causal mechanisms (e.g., 'small boat missed because clutter features dominated'). If >5% of failure cases lack credible explanation (feature importance does not justify model decision), those cases are flagged as 'unexplained' and trigger either model retraining (if cause is known) or ODD restriction (if cause is unknown).
V.acceptance_criterion: When Phase 3 explainability analysis completes, for every model failure (detection miss or misclassification) in the ≥5000-sample test set, an explainability trace (SHAP + Grad-CAM + feature importance) shall be generated. A failure is 'explained' if a qualified SME confirms feature importance aligns with causal narrative (signed-off in evidence log). Fail if >5% of failures lack explanation or if explanation-to-decision linkage is broken. Count: allow ≤250 unexplained failures in 5000-sample set.
V.reasoning: 'Unexplained' is subjective—does it mean 'no SHAP trace' or 'SHAP trace disagrees with narrative'? The criterion must specify what constitutes an explanation and who validates it.
V.recommendation: Create an 'Explainability Validation Rubric' (e.g., SHAP feature rank must match causal domain model, Grad-CAM saliency must overlap with object bounding box). Have a domain expert (maritme sensor SME) sign-off on each 'explained' failure.

V.finding_title: Degraded-sensor fallback response lacks quantified performance threshold
V.original_requirement: degraded-sensor modes trigger graceful fallback (increased caution)
V.verifiable_rewrite: When any primary sensor (camera, LiDAR, or radar) is offline or degraded (signal-to-noise ratio <10 dB for radar, pixel dropout >20% for camera, range <100 m for LiDAR), the collision-avoidance stack shall switch to a conservative mode: (1) detection confidence threshold increased to ≥0.9 (was ≥0.5), (2) collision-avoidance safety margin increased from 500 m to 750 m, (3) operator is alerted within 500 ms.
V.acceptance_criterion: In Phase 4 HIL degraded-sensor scenarios (GPS denial, LiDAR dropout >30 s, camera loss >20%), when the system detects sensor degradation, then (1) mode switch completes within 500 ms, (2) detection confidence threshold transitions from ≥0.5 to ≥0.9, (3) collision-avoidance margins increase from 500 m to 750 m nominal range, (4) operator alert issued within 500 ms. Test 10 degradation scenarios (each sensor, various durations). Fail if any scenario shows delayed switch or incorrect threshold.
V.reasoning: 'Graceful fallback' and 'increased caution' are vague—no target performance or timeline specified. A measurable criterion must define specific thresholds (confidence, range, response latency).
V.recommendation: Establish a Degraded-Sensor Fallback Specification document defining thresholds for each sensor type and failure mode (e.g., 'LiDAR loss >30 s → confidence ≥0.9, margin +250 m, alert ≤500 ms'). Reference it in the SRS.

V.finding_title: Sim-to-real validation gap criterion lacks ODD stratification
V.verifiable_rewrite: Phase 5 physical trial validation shall compare real-world perception accuracy against Phase 4 digital-twin predictions on the same encounter geometries and ODD conditions. A 'sim-to-real gap' is defined as accuracy drop >10 pp in any ODD cell that was tested in both simulation and reality. If detected, root-cause investigation shall assess: (1) Is the gap ODD-specific (e.g., fog effects not modeled)? (2) Is it model-specific (overfitting to synthetic data)? (3) Can it be mitigated by retraining or ODD restriction?
V.acceptance_criterion: For each Phase 5 physical trial in ODD cell C, when the trial completes, the perception stack's accuracy (per-class mAP@IoU=0.5) shall be compared to the Phase 4 simulation prediction for cell C. If real-world accuracy drops >10 pp below simulation, then within 5 working days, a root-cause analysis shall be filed documenting: (1) likely cause (e.g., sensor drift, weather outside ODD), (2) mitigation (e.g., model retrain, ODD restriction, trial repetition), (3) approval status. Fail if investigation missing or unresolved after 5 working days.
V.reasoning: 'Accuracy drop >10%' is ambiguous: drop from what baseline? Drop in which metric (mAP, recall, precision)? Drop across all classes or per-class? The criterion must specify the metric, baseline (simulation), and investigation SLA.
V.recommendation: Create a 'Sim-to-Real Validation Protocol' specifying: (1) metric (per-class mAP@IoU=0.5), (2) acceptable gap (±10 pp with 95% CI), (3) ODD cells to validate (representative sample, ≥3 cells), (4) RCA process and SLA (5 working days for initial analysis, 10 for decision).

V.finding_title: GSN auto-generation completion criterion lacks quality metrics
V.original_requirement: draft GSN generated within 2 working days post-Phase 5 completion
V.verifiable_rewrite: GSN safety case draft shall be generated automatically by consuming all Phase 3–5 evidence artifacts (test results, bounds, simulation logs, physical trial data, drift logs). Draft shall include: (1) AAES table (6 AMLAS activities × evidence items), (2) GSN claims linked to ≥1 evidence item each, (3) traceability matrix (requirements → test → evidence → claim). Draft completeness: ≥95% of top-level claims have ≥1 supporting evidence item. Draft generation time: ≤48 elapsed hours from Phase 5 completion signal.
V.acceptance_criterion: When Phase 5 physical trials complete, the system shall initiate auto-generation of the GSN draft. The draft shall be output as D-Case Editor XML within 48 elapsed hours. Pass criteria: (1) all 6 AMLAS activities appear in AAES table, (2) ≥95% of top-level claims (e.g., 'collision-avoidance safe under fog') have ≥1 linked evidence item, (3) traceability is bidirectional (requirement ↔ test ↔ evidence ↔ claim), (4) no broken links in XML. Fail if any activity missing, <95% claims linked, or links broken.
V.reasoning: 'Within 2 working days' specifies elapsed time but not quality. A 'generated' draft could be syntactically valid XML with empty claims—not useful for safety case. Must quantify claim completeness and evidence linkage.
V.recommendation: Define 'complete GSN draft' as: (1) D-Case XML schema validation passes, (2) all AMLAS activities populated, (3) ≥95% of claims have ≥1 evidence link with traceability ID, (4) no 'TBD' placeholders in top-level claims. Automate validation in the generation pipeline.

V.finding_title: Live drift detection thresholds (±2σ band) lack statistical justification
V.original_requirement: alert if any variable exceeds ±2σ band for ≥3 consecutive uploads (15-minute window)
V.verifiable_rewrite: Live drift monitoring shall compute a baseline distribution of 3 ODD variables (sea-state, visibility, sea-clutter magnitude) from Phase 4 simulation and Phase 5 real trials. For each variable, a band is defined as baseline mean ± 2 standard deviations (95% confidence interval). Drift alert triggers if variable exits band for ≥3 consecutive uploads (15 min window). Alert confidence: ≥95% that true ODD state has drifted (not measurement noise). Calibration: drift thresholds validated on a held-out ODD change scenario (e.g., weather transition from calm to rough sea) to ensure ≥90% detection rate and ≤5% false-positive rate.
V.acceptance_criterion: During Phase 7 deployment, live drift monitoring shall continuously compute sea-state, visibility, and clutter magnitude every 5 minutes. For each variable, alert triggers if ≥3 consecutive measurements (15 min window) fall outside baseline mean ± 2σ. Before Phase 7 gate, validate drift thresholds on a synthetic scenario inducing known ODD change (e.g., sea state Beaufort 2 → 4 over 30 min). Pass if: (1) drift alert triggers within 20 minutes of ODD transition (≥90% sensitivity), (2) false-positive rate ≤5% on 30-day baseline, (3) operator receives email alert <1 hour after drift trigger. Fail if sensitivity <90% or false-positive rate >5%.
V.reasoning: The ±2σ threshold is standard (95% CI) but may be too lenient for safety-critical drift detection. The criterion must validate that this threshold achieves acceptable sensitivity and specificity for the maritime domain (≥90% detection, ≤5% false-positive).
V.recommendation: Run a Phase 7 pre-deployment validation: simulate ODD transitions (calm → rough sea, clear → fog) and measure drift detection rate and false-positive rate. If thresholds are too loose, tighten to ±1.5σ and re-validate. Document threshold selection rationale in the drift monitoring specification.

V.finding_title: HIL sensor simulation realism lacks fidelity metric
V.original_requirement: physics-realistic sensor feedback from Isaac Sim
V.verifiable_rewrite: Isaac Sim or ROS Gazebo sensor simulation shall produce radar echoes, camera frames, and LiDAR pointclouds that match real sensor characteristics within defined tolerances: (1) radar cross-section (RCS) variance ≤±20% of real reference, (2) camera noise profile (Poisson, read noise) consistent with real sensor models, (3) LiDAR beam divergence and range noise within ±10% of real specifications. Validation: compare 100 simulated frames to real frames from matching ODD conditions using domain metrics (spectral correlation for radar, PSNR for imagery).
V.acceptance_criterion: When HIL simulation is initialized with Isaac Sim/Gazebo for a given ODD condition (e.g., calm sea, merchant vessel 500 m away), and 100 time-steps are executed, then simulated sensor outputs shall be compared to real sensor data from a matching ODD cell. Pass if: (1) radar cross-section correlation ≥0.85 (Pearson), (2) camera frame PSNR ≥25 dB, (3) LiDAR range RMSE ≤2% of measurement. Fail if any metric misses target or simulator crashes. Evidence: sensor comparison report + simulator logs.
V.reasoning: 'Physics-realistic' is subjective without measurement criteria. Fidelity validation requires comparing simulated outputs to real sensor data—domain-specific metrics (RCS, PSNR, range error) are appropriate.
V.recommendation: Establish a 'Simulator Validation Plan' specifying which metrics (RCS, PSNR, RMSE) apply to each sensor type and ODD condition. Run validation on a representative ODD cell sample before Phase 4 begins.

M.finding_title: Synthetic Data Provenance Chain Unverifiable Offline — Blocker for Phase 3 Qualification
M.reasoning: REQ-003 (synthetic data generation) and REQ-014 (MLOps lineage) are interdependent but not formally linked. REQ-003 mandates provenance logging (tool versions, git commits, hyperparameters, seeds). REQ-014 mandates MLOps lineage tracking (model version → dataset version → evidence ID). However, REQ-003 does NOT specify MLflow integration or database format. If synthetic data is generated offline (e.g., batch job in Isaac Sim), provenance metadata can drift or become unverifiable when re-imported. SCEN-005 explicitly flags this risk ('provenance chain cannot be verified').
M.recommendation: Merge REQ-003 and REQ-014 into a unified 'Provenance-Linked Synthetic Data Pipeline' requirement. Mandate that every synthetic frame is tagged with immutable provenance metadata (embedded in image EXIF or MLOps database row) before entering evidence store. Define schema for provenance (JSON or protobuf). Require deterministic seed initialization (git commit → seed → frame hash) so re-runs are reproducible. Add acceptance criterion: 'all synthetic data batches linked to MLflow run_id within 5 minutes of generation'.

M.finding_title: IEEE 830 Section 2 (Overall Description) Severely Underpopulated — Critical Interface & Constraint Gaps
M.reasoning: Lucy's coverage report flags 7 empty subsections in Section 2: 2.1 (System Interfaces), 2.1.2 (Hardware), 2.1.4 (Communications), 2.1.5 (Memory Constraints), 2.1.7 (Site Adaptation), 2.6 (Apportioning). Features describe functional behavior but do NOT specify: (a) Isaac Sim API binding schema, (b) ROS 2 DDS interface, (c) classified-data fabric (DAIS/Crown Hosting), (d) GPU hardware specs, (e) network protocols & encryption, (f) memory/storage budgets, (g) subsystem allocation of accuracy targets (model layer vs data layer vs inference pipeline). Without these, Section 3 requirements are incomplete—implementers cannot code to spec.
M.recommendation: Populate Section 2 with detailed subsections: 2.1.1–2.1.7 per IEEE 830 template. For each, trace to features that imply interface or constraint. For example: REQ-008 (HIL) implies Isaac Sim API spec (§2.1); REQ-015 (RBAC) implies classified-data fabric spec (§2.1.4); REQ-002 (sensor curation) implies data-ingestion interface schema (§2.1.1). Create interface control documents (ICDs) for Isaac Sim, ROS 2, MLOps, DAIS. Define hardware SLA (GPU availability >95%, latency <100ms). This is prerequisite for Section 3 elaboration.

M.finding_title: Organizational Scheme Mismatch: Features Scattered Across Phases Without Clear Section 3 Hierarchy
M.reasoning: 16 features map naturally to 7 PSRQ phases (Phase 1-7), but current feature decomposition lacks formal hierarchical structure. REQ-001 (decomposition) belongs to Phase 1, REQ-002 (sensor curation) to Phase 2, REQ-011 (GSN auto-generation) to Phase 6. Without phase-based subsection structure (3.1–3.7), traceability is opaque and maintainability suffers. IEEE 830 Section 3 requires 'Specific Requirements' organized by a coherent scheme; functional hierarchy (phase-based) is most natural for T&E lifecycle.
M.recommendation: Adopt 'by functional hierarchy (PSRQ phases)' as Section 3 organizational scheme. Create subsections 3.1 (Phase 1)–3.7 (Phase 7), assign REQ-IDs accordingly. Map requirements bidirectionally: requirement → phase → scenarios → acceptance criteria. This aligns with Dstl compliance and supports monthly audit traceability.

M.finding_title: Scenario-to-Feature Mapping Not Bidirectional — 28 Scenarios Scattered Across 16 Features Without Clear Hierarchy
M.reasoning: Iris's scenario list (iris-criteria.json) maps each SCEN to a feature name (e.g., SCEN-001 → 'Automated requirements decomposition'). But mapping is one-way: feature → scenarios. For modifiability, scenario IDs must also be hierarchical within each requirement. Currently SCEN-001, SCEN-002, SCEN-003 all map to REQ-001; SCEN-004–006 to REQ-003. This is correct but not formally expressed in a WBS or sub-requirement numbering scheme. When Section 3 is elaborated, readers will struggle to find 'all scenarios for REQ-005' without manual cross-referencing.
M.recommendation: Restructure scenario IDs to reflect hierarchy: SCEN-001 → REQ-001.1 (parent requirement + scenario ordinal). In Section 3, nest scenarios under parent requirement as 'Acceptance Scenarios' sub-subsections. Create traceability matrix in Appendix: REQ-ID → [Scenario IDs] → Acceptance Criteria → Evidence Artifacts. This supports impact analysis (if REQ-005 changes, which SCEN are affected?) and requirement status tracking.

M.finding_title: Missing Unique IDs for Sub-Features and Acceptance Criteria — Traceability Matrix Cannot Be Fully Populated
M.reasoning: Odin's unambiguous SRS statements are detailed (e.g., REQ-001 lists 4 decomposition activities: parse requirements, map to 106-technique catalogue, generate traceability matrix, output SACE XML). But these sub-activities are not formally IDed as REQ-001.1, REQ-001.2, etc. Similarly, Iris's acceptance criteria (measurement units, thresholds, evidence artifacts) are not individually IDed. When Dstl auditors request 'evidence for REQ-001 activity 3' (traceability matrix), can they find a unique ID in the evidence store? Without sub-IDs, traceability is ambiguous.
M.recommendation: Assign hierarchical IDs to sub-features and acceptance criteria: REQ-001 (parent) → REQ-001.1 (parse), REQ-001.2 (map), REQ-001.3 (generate matrix), REQ-001.4 (output SACE). Similarly, for each SCEN, assign sub-criterion IDs: SCEN-001.AC1 (timing), SCEN-001.AC2 (coverage), etc. Populate traceability matrix: REQ-001.1 → SCEN-001.AC1 → evidence_artifact_URI. This enables automated impact analysis and requirements-change management.

M.finding_title: Adversarial Robustness Bounds Definition Ambiguous — Perturbation Budget Not Aligned Across REQ-005 and Scenarios
M.reasoning: REQ-005 specifies 'perturbation budgets 10% and 20% of pixel range' and acceptance criteria (accuracy drop ≤20 pp at ε=0.1, ≤40 pp at ε=0.2). But SCEN-007 uses notation 'L-infinity ε=8/255 (which is ~3% pixel range, not 10%). SCEN-009 references 'pairwise robustness' combining fog + adversarial patch but does NOT define how combined perturbation budgets compose. This ambiguity will cause re-work during Phase 3 gate: are bounds tested individually (ε=0.1 alone) or combinatorially (ε=0.1 + weather condition simultaneously)?
M.recommendation: Clarify and unify perturbation budget notation in REQ-005 statement: explicitly map ε percentages to pixel-space notation (ε=0.1 → 25.5/255 in standard FGSM libraries). Add sub-requirement for 'Pairwise Robustness Testing' specifying how composite perturbations (weather + adversarial) interact. Define 'combined accuracy drop' metric (do they add or compound multiplicatively?). Document in SACE bounds table template.

M.finding_title: Red-teaming and Explainability Requirements Conditional but Critical for Safety Case — Unstable Tooling Threatens v1.0 Timeline
M.reasoning: REQ-006 (red-teaming with Garak/PyRIT) and REQ-007 (explainability with SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM) are marked 'conditional' and 'should_have' by Natasha, targetable for v1.1 deferral. However, 6 of 28 scenarios (SCEN-027, SCEN-028, SCEN-024, SCEN-025, etc.) are explicitly built on red-teaming and explainability outputs. If these requirements slip to v1.1, Phase 3 evidence will have gaps that Dstl safety case review will challenge. Natasha notes 'tools rapidly evolving' (Garak, PyRIT) — but scheduling and API churn risk is not quantified.
M.recommendation: Elevate REQ-006 and REQ-007 to 'essential, v1.0' with explicit risk acceptance or tool-lock strategy. Define tool versions upfront (Garak ≥0.10, PyRIT ≥0.3.0, SHAP ≥0.43). Alternatively, create v1.0 'lite' evidence path (model-layer metrics REQ-004/005 only) and v1.1 'rich assurance path' (add red-team + explainability). Document decision in Assumptions & Dependencies section (IEEE 830 § 2.5).

M.finding_title: Live Drift Detection Phase 7 Requirements Incomplete — Alert Escalation SOP Undefined
M.reasoning: REQ-013 specifies drift detection on 3 ODD variables (sea state, visibility, sea-clutter) with alert thresholds (±2σ band). But acceptance criterion does NOT specify: (a) escalation logic (SLO-yellow → SLO-red → auto-suppress after 12 h), (b) who receives alerts (Dstl 'team lead' named but no distribution list or SLO on response time), (c) how RAISO override is requested and logged. SCEN-013–015 detail these behaviors but are NOT formalized in REQ-013 statement. This creates ambiguity: if email delivery to Dstl fails, is the requirement failed? Who resolves the alert?
M.recommendation: Expand REQ-013 acceptance criteria to include: (1) alert escalation SOP (SLO-yellow timing, SLO-red threshold, auto-suppress delay), (2) notification routing (Dstl distribution list, email SLA <1 hour), (3) RAISO override request process (who submits, signature requirement, audit trail). Cross-reference SCEN-013–015 in requirement statement. Define fallback behavior if primary comms fails (e.g., SMS backup).

M.finding_title: Phase 4 and Phase 5 Requirements Entangled — HIL Validation and Physical Trials Lack Clear Gate Criteria
M.reasoning: REQ-008 (HIL & digital-twin evaluation, Phase 4) and REQ-010 (targeted physical trials, Phase 5) both conduct 'collision-avoidance validation'. REQ-008 specifies ≥98% scenario pass rate, latency ≤1 s, degraded-sensor fallback. REQ-010 specifies 'sim-to-real gap' closure (accuracy drop >10 pp triggers investigation). But gate criteria are mixed: can Phase 5 start if Phase 4 shows 95% pass rate (below 98% target)? What if Phase 4 HIL and Phase 5 trials conflict (e.g., HIL shows 98% success but real world shows 60% due to sensor drift)? Which evidence takes precedence for safety case sign-off?
M.recommendation: Formalize gate criteria and precedence rules in Section 3.4–3.5: (1) Phase 4 gate: HIL must achieve ≥95% scenario pass rate (relaxed from 98% as 'confidence metric'; 98% is 'target for production'); go/no-go gate: latency ≤1 s AND degraded-sensor fallback confirmed. (2) Phase 5 gate: real-world trials must confirm sim-to-real accuracy gap ≤10 pp; if >10 pp, RCA required, no go-live until resolved. (3) Precedence: real-world evidence (Phase 5) overrides simulation (Phase 4) for operational release decision. Document in 'Evaluation Phases' subsection.

M.finding_title: Feature Overlap: REQ-005 (Adversarial Bounds) and REQ-006 (Red-teaming) Both Assess Robustness — Boundary Unclear
M.reasoning: REQ-005 conducts 'adversarial robustness testing' with Foolbox (FGSM, PGD, C&W attacks) and files bounds in SACE. REQ-006 conducts 'red-teaming' with PyRIT (spoofing, jamming, occlusion, morphing). Both measure 'accuracy drop under adversarial conditions'. Difference is subtle: REQ-005 is model-layer testing (pixel-space perturbations), REQ-006 is scenario-based testing (system-level stressors). But overlap in scope and tooling creates risk of double-testing or inconsistent results. If REQ-005 and REQ-006 produce conflicting bounds (e.g., REQ-005 says ≥87% robust, REQ-006 scenarios show >30 pp drop), which takes precedence for safety case?
M.recommendation: Clarify boundary in requirements: REQ-005 is 'Adversarial Perturbation Testing (model-layer)' with focus on pixel-space attacks. REQ-006 is 'Adversarial Scenario Testing (system-level)' with focus on physical-world conditions (fog, jamming, etc.). Add cross-reference: REQ-005 results inform perturbation budgets for REQ-006 scenarios. Define conflict resolution: if REQ-006 scenario uncovers worse degradation than REQ-005 bound predicts, escalate to algorithm hardening investigation.

M.finding_title: RAISO Override Authority and Scope Not Formally Specified — Creates Ambiguity in Exception Handling
M.reasoning: REQ-015 (RBAC) names 'RAISO' as an access role with 'override authority on exceptions'. REQ-011 mentions 'RAISO for exception' in GSN rejection scenarios (SCEN-017). REQ-013 (drift detection) specifies RAISO override for out-of-bounds operation. But requirements do NOT specify: (a) what constitutes an 'exception' (individual requirement failure? Systemic issue?), (b) RAISO approval criteria (subjective risk assessment? Quantified bounds?), (c) escalation timeline (how long can operation continue under RAISO override before retraining mandatory?), (d) audit trail (who signs off, when, with what justification?). This creates ambiguity that will delay sea trials during Phase 5.
M.recommendation: Add explicit 'Exception Handling & RAISO Override' requirement (e.g., REQ-015.1) specifying: (1) exception categories (e.g., 'test failure', 'evidence gap', 'ODD exceedance'), (2) RAISO approval criteria (e.g., 'risk-based justification + schedule impact <5 days'), (3) override duration limit (e.g., 'override valid for 10 working days; retraining or ODD restriction required after'), (4) audit trail format (PDF approval + git commit with signature). Link to SCEN-015 (auto-suppress after 12 h of unresolved alert) and SCEN-017 (GSN revision after rejection).

T.finding_title: REQ-015 + REQ-016 are regulatory non-negotiables with zero flexibility
T.reasoning: RBAC (REQ-015) and CI/CD (REQ-016) are marked essential with stable, no CRITICAL findings. These are blocking gates: Dstl cannot approve evidence without RBAC compliance (DEFSTAN 00-056), and monthly audits require automated CI/CD traceability.
T.recommendation: Prioritize REQ-015/016 infrastructure in sprints 1-2. Both must be live before Phase 2 data ingestion.

T.finding_title: Dependency chain: REQ-001 → REQ-002/003 → REQ-004/005 → REQ-010 → REQ-011/012 forms critical path
T.reasoning: Phase sequencing is strict: Phase 1 decomposition (REQ-001) must complete before Phase 2 data curation (REQ-002/003); Phase 3 evaluation (REQ-004/005) requires Phase 2 data; Phase 5 validation (REQ-010) requires Phase 4 scenarios (REQ-008/009); Phase 6 evidence bundle (REQ-011/012) requires all prior phases. Any slip cascades.
T.recommendation: Create Phase-level dependencies in CI/CD gating. Enforce serial execution of phase gates. Flag critical path in project plan: Phase 1 (T+30d) → Phase 2 (T+60d) → Phase 3 (T+90d) → Phase 4 (T+120d) → Phase 5 (T+150d) → Phase 6 (T+180d).

T.finding_title: REQ-002 synthetic data provenance chain unverifiable offline (CRITICAL in Natasha ranking)
T.reasoning: REQ-003 (Synthetic data generation) carries CRITICAL finding re: provenance chain. MLflow tracking is non-negotiable blocker. Without REQ-014 (MLOps lineage), provenance is unauditable, blocking Dstl qualification.
T.recommendation: Enforce MLflow + DVC tracking on ALL synthetic data jobs. Implement immutable hash store for provenance validation. Integrate in Phase 2 data-ingestion contract.

T.finding_title: REQ-004 + REQ-005 metric definition blockers cascade to Phase 3 gate
T.reasoning: Natasha ranked REQ-004/005 as carrying CRITICAL findings (metric definition, test-set split). Without Navy sign-off on mAP threshold + adversarial epsilon bounds in Phase 1, Phase 3 verification cannot proceed.
T.recommendation: Lock Phase 1 requirements outputs before Phase 2 data work begins. Create Dstl-approved metric charter in kickoff meeting.

T.finding_title: REQ-013 + REQ-014 incomplete; Phase 7 design defers to v1.1
T.reasoning: REQ-013 (Live drift detection) and REQ-014 (MLOps lineage) are essential but Natasha ranked them with CRITICAL + MAJOR findings. Drift metric definition and escalation SOP are unfinished. Phase 7 can be implemented post-sea-trial; v1.0 gates on Phase 6 evidence.
T.recommendation: Explicitly defer Phase 7 operationalization to v1.1. Lock Phase 6 evidence archival interface (write-once, read-audit). Define drift re-qualification trigger in Phase 7 design review post-sea-trial.

T.finding_title: REQ-006 + REQ-009 are should_have and deferrable to v1.1 if Phase 3 schedule slips
T.reasoning: Red-teaming (REQ-006, volatile) and Scenic automation (REQ-009, moderate) are marked should_have by Natasha. If Phase 3 timeline tightens, both can be deferred; core safety case stands on REQ-004/005 alone.
T.recommendation: Define Phase 3 critical path: REQ-001 → REQ-002 → REQ-003 → REQ-004 + REQ-005. Flag REQ-006/009 as optional in Phase 3 PSRQ gate criteria.

T.finding_title: REQ-010 (Phase 5 physical trials) depends on REQ-008 (Phase 4 HIL) outcome; circular if trials fail
T.reasoning: Phase 5 (REQ-010) is validation anchor for Phase 4 (REQ-008) digital-twin results. If Phase 5 trials uncover >10pp sim-to-real gap (SCEN-022), then Phase 4 evidence is downgraded, requiring Phase 3 model retraining (REQ-004). Circular: evidence→validation→evidence.
T.recommendation: Define sim-to-real acceptance thresholds in Phase 4 test design. If Phase 5 gap >5pp, trigger Phase 3 RCA immediately (not post-Phase-6). Lock Phase 4 scenarios before Phase 5 planning.

T.finding_title: No traceability from standards (AMLAS, SACE, GSN) to specific acceptance criteria
T.reasoning: Each REQ cites standards (e.g., REQ-001 cites AMLAS Phase 1), but Iris acceptance criteria do not explicitly map to AMLAS activities (A1–A6). GSN claims are auto-generated (REQ-011) but baseline claim structure is not specified.
T.recommendation: Create standards-to-PSRQ mapping: AMLAS A1 (specification of acceptable behaviour) → Phase 1 + REQ-001; A2 (training data) → Phase 2 + REQ-002; etc. Validate GSN auto-generation templates against DEFSTAN 00-056 patterns.

T.finding_title: No requirements trace REQ-012 (Article 36 export) to deployment decision
T.reasoning: REQ-012 exports evidence for Article 36 legal review, but no forward trace specifies operational decision gates (approved → deployment authorized, conditional → escalate to RAISO, rejected → halt). Evidence bundle exists in abstract; legal signoff is unclear.
T.recommendation: Define REQ-012 forward trace to deployment authorization logic. Create decision matrix: Article 36 approval status → deployment SLA. Integrate with REQ-016 (CI/CD gate).

T.finding_title: REQ-007 (Explainability) is conditional; no forward trace if deferred
T.reasoning: REQ-007 is marked should_have (conditional necessity). Iris created SCEN-024/025 for explainability, but if REQ-007 is deferred, those scenarios are orphaned. No test cases specify fallback (ODD restriction instead of explainability proof).
T.recommendation: If REQ-007 deferred, archive SCEN-024/025 as optional evidence. Update Phase 3 acceptance criteria to clarify explainability as optional if model-layer accuracy passes REQ-004/005 alone.
